lecture_topic_summary: Nominally a deformation processing lecture; in practice a discursive lecture on the gap between technical knowledge and business decision-making. Tom uses three principal cases: (1) the high-temperature superconductor bandwagon of 1987-1990 and his contrarian stance on critical current density; (2) the all-aluminum automobile prediction of the early 1990s and the body-in-white cost analysis (Joel Clark's student's thesis); (3) the Armco Steel ingot-vs-continuous-casting decision of the 1960s as an instance of risk-averse Harvard-Business-School-trained executives. Frame story: Kim Clark (then HBS dean) and Tom's contrasting institutional experiences. Closing argument: MIT trains technicians, not leaders; the leaders come from elsewhere (HBS) and make bad decisions about the technology MIT graduates understand.
principal_cases: High-temperature superconductors (1987-1990 bandwagon, T-sub-c vs critical current density vs critical magnetic field)
All-aluminum automobile prediction (early 1990s, Ford Taurus body-in-white cost comparison)
Armco Steel ingot casting vs continuous casting decision (mid-1960s)
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing leadership succession (Morris Chang, Nun-Sen Tsai)
American Superconductor spin-off (Greg Yurek, Vander Sande, Chiang)
principal_figures_mentioned: Jim Williams (Ohio State, Carnegie Mellon, GE)
Bob Brown (MIT Dean of Engineering, later BU President)
tom_misstatements_flagged: p10: '1988' Nobel for high-Tc superconductivity was actually 1987 (Bednorz & Müller) — preserved per constitutional constraint
p25: '$115,000 all-aluminum Taurus' captioner error for '$15,000 [steel] Taurus' — bracketed
notes_for_editor: This lecture is largely off-topic from deformation processing — Tom acknowledges this explicitly in the closing (p37). The dislocations historical material in p1-p6 is the most on-topic content. The body-in-white aluminum cost analysis (p23-p25) is the deformation-processing-adjacent case. The Kim Clark / HBS / Armco material (p28-p32) and the TSMC/Nun-Sen material (p33-p35) are pure career-philosophy material. Recommend dividing layer 3 into clear sections: (s1) Hertzberg/Backofen/dislocation history, (s2) The superconductor bandwagon and Tom's contrarian stance, (s3) The American Superconductor spin-off and the Cima/Brown episode, (s4) The all-aluminum automobile prediction and body-in-white economics, (s5) Kim Clark, Armco, and the HBS critique, (s6) TSMC and the technicians-vs-leaders closing.
